channel.title: Course Reference on RaihnForge
channel.description: Recent content in Course Reference on RaihnForge

item.title: ADMG 490 — Cooperative Education
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Full title. Cooperative Education.
  • Catalog description. Supervised work experience that applies classroom learning in a professional setting. Students earn academic credit for structured, mentored work in an industry-aligned role. Drawn from the CWU course catalog entry for ADMG 490.
  • Credits. Variable, typically 4 to 12 credits depending on the agreed scope of the work experience and hours logged per term.
  • Prerequisites. Department permission. Students usually need standing in the Administrative Management major and a formal learning agreement signed by both a workplace mentor and a faculty supervisor before enrolling.
  • Designation. Major-track experiential credit. ADMG 490 is the Administrative Management version of CWU’s cooperative-education offering. The Information Technology and Administrative Management department also runs IT 490A on the IT track. Students in dual-track programs sometimes carry both, applying the same workplace hours through different academic lenses.
  • Thematic frame. Bridging classroom theory and professional practice. The course treats the workplace as the curriculum, with faculty and supervisor partnership as the mechanism for translating raw experience into structured learning.

Official Learning Outcomes

Paraphrased from CWU cooperative-education materials. On completion, a student should be able to:

item.title: IT 202 — Change Ready: Technology Skills for Civic and Community Leaders
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Full title. Change Ready: Technology Skills for Civic and Community Leaders.
  • Catalog description. Develops practical productivity-software competency in Microsoft Word, Excel, Access, and PowerPoint, framed around the technology demands of civic and community leadership work. Paraphrased from the CWU course catalog.
  • Credits. 5
  • Prerequisites. None listed. The course is positioned as a general-education and ITAM-foundation offering open to students across majors.
  • Designation. Core requirement in the CWU Information Technology and Administrative Management program. The course also satisfies a general-education breadth requirement in several program plans.
  • Thematic frame. Office-suite literacy reframed as civic infrastructure. The course’s stated frame is that nonprofit boards, neighborhood associations, city advisory committees, and community organizations all run on the same handful of productivity tools, and competence in those tools is a precondition for effective civic participation.

Official Learning Outcomes

Paraphrased from CWU course-catalog and ITAM program materials. On completion, a student should be able to:

item.title: IT 248 — Web Fundamentals
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Full title. Web Fundamentals.
  • Catalog description. Introductory course in web development covering HTML structure, CSS styling, and the core practices of building accessible, standards-based web pages. Paraphrased from the CWU course catalog.
  • Credits. 4 to 5, depending on catalog year.
  • Prerequisites. None listed at the introductory level. Some sections recommend prior exposure to file systems and basic computing concepts from IT 101 or equivalent.
  • Designation. Core requirement in the CWU Information Technology and Administrative Management program. The course is the on-ramp for the web-focused upper-division IT and ITAM electives.
  • Thematic frame. Building for the open web. The course treats HTML and CSS as the two foundational languages of the public internet and trains students to write both by hand before any framework, content-management system, or builder tool enters the conversation.

Official Learning Outcomes

Paraphrased from CWU course-catalog and ITAM program materials. On completion, a student should be able to:

item.title: CS 110 — Programming Fundamentals 1
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Catalog description. “Fundamental concepts of programming from an object-oriented perspective. Classes, objects and methods, algorithm development, problem-solving techniques, basic control structures, primitive types and arrays.”
  • Credits. 4
  • General-education designation. None. CS 110 is a major-track foundation course, not a gen-ed offering.
  • Prerequisites. None listed in the catalog. Students typically come in with high-school-algebra-level math fluency and no prior programming required.
  • Approach. Object-oriented from the start. CWU’s CS department teaches CS 110 in Java, with classes, objects, and methods introduced alongside the standard imperative constructs rather than after them. The course is the on-ramp to CS 111 (Programming Fundamentals II) and the rest of the Computer Science major sequence.

Official Learning Outcomes

Quoted from the CWU course catalog. Upon successful completion, students will demonstrate the ability to:

item.title: ENG 104 — Academic Writing II: Sustainability & Civic Engagement
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Full title. Academic Writing II: Reasoning and Research on Sustainability and Civic Engagement.
  • Catalog description. “Develops skills in research-based academic argument through assignments involving evaluation, analysis, and synthesis of multiple sources.” Offered every year (Fall, Winter, Spring).
  • Credits. 5
  • Prerequisites. ENG 101 with a grade of C- or higher.
  • General-education designation. K1, Academic Writing II: Critical Reading and Responding. One of the required Knowledge Areas in the Explore and Connect section of the CWU General Education program.
  • Thematic frame. Sustainability and civic engagement. The course is one of three thematic ENG 104 variants offered alongside ENG 102 (Social Justice) and ENG 103 (Health and Current Issues); all three teach the same core competencies through a different topical lens.

Official Learning Outcomes

Quoted from the CWU course catalog. Upon completion, a student should be able to:

item.title: MATH 100A — Pre-Algebra
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Catalog description. “This course is designed to prepare students for college mathematics. Symbolic, graphical, and numeric representations will be studied to understand and apply the concepts underlying algebra.”
  • Credits. 5.
  • General-education designation. None. Per the catalog: “Grade will be XC. Credits will not be allowed toward meeting bachelor’s degree requirements.” Developmental / preparatory standing. The course appears on the transcript but does not count toward the bachelor’s degree.
  • Prerequisites. “Prerequisite: appropriate placement test score.”
  • Offered. Ellensburg campus, Fall, Winter, Spring.
  • Approach. A bridge course. The job is to repair, consolidate, and re-frame arithmetic so the student can step into MATH 100B (Beginning Algebra) and onward without the foundational gaps that derail most college-algebra attempts.

Official Learning Outcomes

Quoted from the CWU course catalog. Upon successful completion, students will be able to:

item.title: RELS 101 — World Religions
item.description:
Course Snapshot

  • Institution. Central Washington University.
  • Catalog description. “Survey of the major world religions (Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, Daoism), including their tenets, practices, and evaluation of the human condition.”
  • Credits. 5
  • General-education designation. AH-Philosophies and Culture World (W); K5 — Humanities. Pathway: P6 Ways of Knowing.
  • Prerequisites. None.
  • Approach. The CWU Comparative Religion program describes itself as offering “a strong, pluralistic foundation in philosophy and religion” with notable departmental expertise in Asian philosophy and religion, so the Eastern traditions tend to receive proportionally rich treatment alongside the Western ones.

Official Learning Outcomes

Quoted from the CWU course catalog. Upon completion, a student should be able to:
